title: Try the Oldest Known Recipe For Toothpaste: From Ancient Egypt, Circa the 4th Century BC
description:
Image of Ancient EgyptôÙian DenôÙtistry, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
When we assume that modôÙern improveôÙments are far supeôÙriôÙor to the pracôÙtices of
body:
Image of Ancient EgyptôÙian DenôÙtistry, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
When we assume that modôÙern improveôÙments are far supeôÙriôÙor to the pracôÙtices of the ancients, we might do well to actuôÙalôÙly learn how peoôÙple in the disôÙtant past lived before indulging in ãchronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal snobôÙbery.ã Take, for examôÙple, the area of denôÙtal hygiene. We might imagôÙine the ancient Greeks or EgypôÙtians as prone to ramôÙpant tooth decay, lackôÙing the benôÙeôÙfits of packôÙaged, brandôÙed toothôÙpaste, silken ribôÙbons of floss, astrinôÙgent mouthôÙwash, and ergonomôÙic toothôÙbrushôÙes. But in fact, as toothôÙpaste manôÙuôÙfacôÙturôÙer ColôÙgate points out, ãthe basic funôÙdaôÙmenôÙtalsã of toothôÙbrush design ãhave not changed since the times of the EgypôÙtians and Babyloniansãa hanôÙdle to grip, and a brisôÙtle-like feaôÙture with which to clean the teeth.ã And not only did ancient peoôÙple use toothôÙbrushôÙes, but it is believed that ãEgypôÙtiansãÎ startôÙed using a paste to clean their teeth around 5000 BC,ã even before toothôÙbrushôÙes were inventôÙed.
In 2003, curaôÙtors at a VienôÙnese museôÙum disôÙcovôÙered ãthe worldãs oldôÙest-known forôÙmuôÙla for toothôÙpaste,ã writes Irene Zoech in The TeleôÙgraph, ãused more than 1,500 years before ColôÙgate began marôÙketôÙing the first comôÙmerôÙcial brand in 1873.ã DatôÙing from the 4th cenôÙtuôÙry AD, the EgyptôÙian papyrus (not shown above), writôÙten in Greek, describes a ãpowôÙder for white and perôÙfect teethã that, when mixed with saliôÙva, makes a ãclean tooth paste.ã The recipe is as folôÙlows, Zoech sumôÙmaôÙrizes: ããÎone drachôÙma of rock saltãmeasure equal to one hunôÙdredth of an ounceãtwo drachôÙmas of mint, one drachôÙma of dried iris flower and 20 grains of pepôÙper, all of them crushed and mixed togethôÙer.ã
Zoech quotes DenôÙtist Heinz NeuôÙman, who remarked, ãNobody in the denôÙtal proôÙfesôÙsion had any idea that such an advanced toothôÙpaste forôÙmuôÙla of this antiqôÙuiôÙty existôÙed.ã HavôÙing tried the ancient recipe at a denôÙtal conôÙferôÙence in AusôÙtria, he found it ãnot unpleasôÙantã
It was painful on my gums and made them bleed as well, but thatãs not a bad thing, and afterôÙwards my mouth felt fresh and clean. I believe that this recipe would have been a big improveôÙment on some of the soap toothôÙpastes used much latôÙer.
DisôÙcovôÙered among ãthe largest colôÙlecôÙtion of ancient EgyptôÙian docôÙuôÙments in the world,ã the docôÙuôÙment, says HerôÙmann HarôÙrauer, head of the papyrus colôÙlecôÙtion at the NationôÙal Library in VienôÙna, ãwas writôÙten by someôÙone whoãs obviôÙousôÙly had some medôÙical knowlôÙedge, as he used abbreôÙviôÙaôÙtions for medôÙical terms.ã
When we surôÙveyô the denôÙtal remeôÙdies of Medieval EngôÙland, we do indeed find that modôÙern denôÙtal care is far betôÙter thanô much of what was availôÙable then. Most denôÙtal cures of the time, writes Trevor AnderôÙson in a Nature artiôÙcle, ãwere based on herbal remeôÙdies, charms and amulets.ã For examôÙple, inô the 1314ô Rosa AngliôÙca, writer John of GadôÙdesôÙden reports, ãsome say that the beak of a magôÙpie hung from the neck cures pain in the teeth.ã AnothôÙer remôÙeôÙdy involves stickôÙing a neeôÙdle into a ãmany footôÙed worm which rolls up in a ball when you touch it.ã Touch the aching tooth with that roly-poly neeôÙdle and ãthe pain will be erased.ã
HowôÙevôÙer, ãthere is also docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary eviôÙdence,ã writes AnderôÙson, ãfor powôÙders to clean teeth and attempts at fillôÙing carôÙiôÙous cavôÙiôÙties,ã as well as some surôÙgiôÙcal interôÙvenôÙtion. In GilberôÙtus AngliôÙcusã 13th cenôÙtuôÙry ComôÙpendiôÙum of MedôÙiôÙcine, readôÙers are told to rub teeth and gums with cloth after eatôÙing to ensure that ãno corôÙrupt matôÙter abides among the teeth.ã In The TroôÙtuôÙlaãa comôÙpendiôÙum of folk remeôÙdies from the 11th or 12th centuryãwe find many recipes for what we might conôÙsidôÙer toothôÙpaste, though their effiôÙcaôÙcy is dubiôÙous. Daniû´le CybulôÙskie at Medievalists.net quotes one recipe ãfor black teethã:
ãÎtake walôÙnut shells well cleaned of the inteôÙriôÙor rind, which is green, andãÎ rub the teeth three times a day, and when they have been well rubbedãÎ wash the mouth with warm wine, and with salt mixed if desired.
AnothôÙer, more extravôÙaôÙgant, recipe sounds impracôÙtiôÙcal.
Take burnt white marôÙble and burnt date pits, and white natron, a red tile, salt, and pumice. From all of these make a powôÙder in which damp wool has been wrapped in a fine linen cloth. Rub the teeth inside and out.
Yet a third recipe gives us a luxôÙuôÙry variôÙety, its ingreôÙdiôÙents well out of reach of the averôÙage perôÙson. We are assured, howôÙevôÙer, that this forôÙmuôÙla ãworks the best.ã
Take some each of cinôÙnaôÙmon, clove, spikeôÙnard, masôÙtic, frankôÙinôÙcense, grain, wormôÙwood, crab foot, date pits, and olives. Grind all of these and reduce them to a powôÙder, then rub the affectôÙed places.
Whether any of these forôÙmuôÙlasô would have worked at all, I canôÙnot say, but they likeôÙly worked betôÙter than charms and amulets. In any case, whileô medieval EuroôÙpean texts tend to conôÙfirm cerôÙtain of our ideas about poor denôÙtal hygiene of the past, it seems that the daiôÙly pracôÙtices of more ancient peoôÙples in Egypt and elseôÙwhere might have been much more like our own thanô we would susôÙpect.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Why the Ancient Romans Had BetôÙter Teeth Than ModôÙern EuroôÙpeans
Who RealôÙly Built the EgyptôÙian PyramidsãAnd How Did They Do It?
DisôÙcovôÙer the OldôÙest Beer Recipe in HisôÙtoôÙry From Ancient SumeôÙria, 1800 B.C.
An Ancient EgyptôÙian HomeôÙwork AssignôÙment from 1800 Years Ago: Some Things Are TruôÙly TimeôÙless
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Greatest Documentary Youãve Never Heard Of: An Introduction to Wang Bingãs Nine-Hour Tie Xi Qu
description:
in Film |   	April 21st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ChiôÙnese filmôÙmakôÙer Wang Bingãs ãTil MadôÙness Do Us Part, a docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary about a men
body:
in Film |   	April 21st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The ChiôÙnese filmôÙmakôÙer Wang Bingãs ãTil MadôÙness Do Us Part, a docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary about a menôÙtal instiôÙtuôÙtion in YunôÙnan, runs three hours and 48 minôÙutes. BeauôÙty Lives in FreeôÙdom, on the life of imprisôÙoned artist Gao Ertai, is five and a half hours long;ô Dead Souls, on the surôÙvivors of a hard-labor camp in the Gobi Desert, eight hours and fifôÙteen minôÙutes. Even if you know nothôÙing else of his work, you may get the impresôÙsion that Wang isnãt the most shameôÙlessôÙly comôÙmerôÙcial of filmôÙmakôÙers. The extreme duraôÙtion of some of his movies sureôÙly make them a hard sell, as do his grim choicôÙes of subôÙject matôÙter. But if you want to underôÙstand the transôÙforôÙmaôÙtion of modôÙern ChiôÙna, you could hardôÙly find a richôÙer body of cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic work.
In the video essay above, YouTuôÙber Ken Dai extols the virtues of Wangãs first film: Tie Xi Qu: West of the Tracks, whose more than nine hours of footage depict the last years of the titôÙuôÙlar indusôÙtriôÙal disôÙtrict of Shenyang. Wang draws them from the more than 300 hours he shot in the years between 1999 and 2001, by which time a shift in ecoôÙnomôÙic polôÙiôÙcy had made redunôÙdant what had once been not just a conôÙcenôÙtraôÙtion of state-owned enterôÙprisôÙes, but ãa monôÙuôÙment to a vision of the future.ã
Tie Xi employed countôÙless many in the foundries and facôÙtoôÙries that made posôÙsiôÙble the draôÙmatôÙic earôÙly decades of ChiôÙnaãs ecoôÙnomôÙic rise, but for its workôÙers and their famôÙiôÙlies alike, it had also become a stage on which genôÙerôÙaôÙtions of life played out.
Wang bears witôÙness to that stageãs disôÙmanôÙtleôÙment. In the filmãs first part, Dai says, ãwe watch the workôÙers show up, day after day, to a sysôÙtem that has already decidôÙed theyãre no longer necôÙesôÙsary.ã The secôÙond turns to ãthe famôÙiôÙlies, and parôÙticôÙuôÙlarôÙly the teenagersã; the third ãfolôÙlows a freight railôÙway that once conôÙnectôÙed all of it, and two men, a son and a father, who live and scavôÙenge for scrap metôÙals.ã They and the many othôÙer remainôÙing Tie Xi denizens who pass before Wangãs camôÙera speak for themôÙselves. At no point does the film incorôÙpoôÙrate narôÙraôÙtion, interôÙviews, or even non-diegetic music. (There is, howôÙevôÙer, an imprompôÙtu perôÙforôÙmance by a nude guiôÙtar-playôÙing man in a barôÙracks.) In its refusal to use its peoôÙple as metaphorôÙiôÙcal figôÙures or politôÙiôÙcal props, Tie Xi Qu stands as an examôÙple ofô ãdirect cinôÙeôÙmaã at its most direct ã except, perôÙhaps, for Wangãs latôÙer clothôÙing-facôÙtoôÙry docôÙuôÙmenôÙtary, the aptôÙly titled 15 Hours.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
285 Free DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries Online
50 Must-See DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries, SelectôÙed by 10 InfluôÙenôÙtial DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary FilmôÙmakôÙers
A ChiôÙnese Painter SpeôÙcialôÙizôÙing in CopyôÙing Van Gogh PaintôÙings TravôÙels to AmsôÙterôÙdam & Sees Van Goghãs MasôÙterôÙpieces for the First Time
The GodôÙdess: A ClasôÙsic from the GoldôÙen Age of ChiôÙnese CinôÙeôÙma, StarôÙring the Silent Film Icon Ruan Lingyu (1934)
Chinaãs 8,000 TerôÙraôÙcotôÙta WarôÙriors: An AniôÙmatôÙed & InterôÙacôÙtive IntroôÙducôÙtion to a Great ArchaeôÙoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal DisôÙcovôÙery
Watch the Film That InventôÙed CinôÙeôÙma: WorkôÙers LeavôÙing the Lumiû´re FacôÙtoôÙry in Lyon (1895)
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: The $666 Board That Built Apple: How the Apple I Changed Computing 50 Years Ago
description:
in History, Technology |   	April 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

AmerôÙiôÙcans of a cerôÙtain age may well rememôÙber growôÙing up with an Apple I
body:
in History, Technology |   	April 20th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

AmerôÙiôÙcans of a cerôÙtain age may well rememôÙber growôÙing up with an Apple II in the classôÙroom, and the perôÙpetôÙuôÙal tempôÙtaôÙtion it held out to play The OreôÙgon Trail,ô NumôÙber MunchôÙers, or perôÙhapsô Lode RunôÙner. More than a few recess gamers went on to comôÙputôÙer-oriôÙentôÙed careers, but only the most curiôÙous sought an answer to the quesôÙtion implied in the machineãs name: was there an Apple I? Half a cenôÙtuôÙry after the founôÙdaôÙtion of Apple, Inc., then known as Apple ComôÙputôÙer, the prodôÙuct that launched whatãs now one of the worldãs most valuôÙable comôÙpaôÙnies remains very much an obscuôÙriôÙty. Unless you freôÙquent comôÙputôÙer museôÙums, youãre unlikeôÙly ever to have laid eyes on an Apple I, let alone used one. Even if one of the existôÙing modôÙels were to come on the marôÙket, youãd need about half a milôÙlion dolôÙlars to buy it.


	

Itãs actuôÙalôÙly easôÙiôÙer to buy the parts that went into an Apple I and build it yourôÙself ã which, as demonôÙstratôÙed by the 8ãBit Guy in the video above, still isnãt easy at all. Yet it does conôÙvey someôÙthing of what Appleãs very first cusôÙtomers would have expeôÙriôÙenced in 1976, when do-it-yourôÙself was the order of the day in comôÙputôÙing.
When I bought the MacôÙBook on which Iãm writôÙing this post, I simôÙply opened it up and, natôÙuôÙralôÙly, found it ready to use. That would scarceôÙly have been imagôÙinôÙable to comôÙputôÙer enthuôÙsiôÙasts of the mid-sevôÙenôÙties, accusôÙtomed as they were to solôÙderôÙing indiôÙvidôÙuôÙalôÙly purôÙchased chips onto elecôÙtronôÙics boards by hand. The Apple I marked a great leap forôÙward in conôÙveôÙnience by comôÙing already assemôÙbled, albeit withôÙout a monôÙiôÙtor, a keyôÙboard, or even a case; the purôÙchase price of USD $666.66 (closôÙer to $4,000 today) just got you the board. But what a board.

Though we rememôÙber Steve Jobs as the masôÙterôÙmind, the Apple I is a tour de force of the engiôÙneerôÙing genius of his busiôÙness partôÙner Steve WozôÙniôÙak. When the Steves debuted it at the HomeôÙbrew ComôÙputôÙer Club in July of 1976, the relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly small numôÙber of chips and advanced funcôÙtions (BASIC proôÙgramôÙming! CasôÙsette-tape data storôÙage! ActuôÙal video outôÙput, if only of teleôÙtype-like scrolling text!) creôÙatôÙed a conôÙsidôÙerôÙable demand then and there. We often hear of Jobs and WozôÙniôÙak startôÙing Apple in a garage, and it was in that garage (as well as the houseôÙãs livôÙing room) that the first Apple I boards were put togethôÙer. UltiôÙmateôÙly, 200 were sold before the Apple II arrived the folôÙlowôÙing year. Appleãs first comôÙputôÙer may look intimôÙiôÙdatôÙing to most of todayãs Mac users. But conôÙsidôÙer the comôÙpaôÙnyãs repôÙuôÙtaôÙtion for minôÙiôÙmalôÙism, accesôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty, and a knack for capôÙturôÙing the conôÙsumerãs imagôÙiôÙnaôÙtion: all qualôÙiôÙties present on that board 50 years ago.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
RidôÙley Scott on the MakôÙing of Appleãs IconôÙic ã1984ã ComôÙmerôÙcial, Aired on Super Bowl SunôÙday in 1984
Before The SimpôÙsons, Matt GroenôÙing IllusôÙtratôÙed a ãStudentãs Guideã for Apple ComôÙputôÙers (1989)
Hunter S. Thompsonãs Edgy 1990s ComôÙmerôÙcial for Appleãs MacôÙinôÙtosh ComôÙputôÙer: A MedôÙiôÙtaôÙtion on PowôÙer
DisôÙcovôÙered: The User ManôÙuôÙal for the OldôÙest SurôÙvivôÙing ComôÙputôÙer in the World
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Gandhi Writes Letters to Hitler: ãWe Have Found in Non-Violence a Force Which Can Match the Most Violent Forces in the Worldã (1939/40)
description:
Image via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
It must come up in every sinôÙgle arguôÙment, from sophisôÙtiôÙcatôÙed to sophoôÙmoric, about the pracôÙtiôÙcaôÙbilôÙiôÙty of
body:
Image via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
It must come up in every sinôÙgle arguôÙment, from sophisôÙtiôÙcatôÙed to sophoôÙmoric, about the pracôÙtiôÙcaôÙbilôÙiôÙty of non-vioôÙlent paciôÙfism. ãLook what GandôÙhi and MarôÙtin Luther King, Jr. were able to achieve!ã ãYes, but what about Hitler? What do you do about the Nazis?ã The rebutôÙtal implies future Nazi-like entiôÙties loomôÙing on the horiôÙzon, and though this reducôÙtio ad Hitlerum genôÙerôÙalôÙly has the effect of nulôÙliôÙfyôÙing any conôÙtinôÙued ratioôÙnal disôÙcusôÙsion, itãs difôÙfiôÙcult to imagôÙine a satôÙisôÙfyôÙing paciôÙfist answer to the probôÙlem of naked, implacaôÙble hatred and aggresôÙsion on such a scale as that of the Third Reich. Even GandôÙhiãs own proôÙposôÙal sounds like a joke: in 1940, Adolf Hitler abanôÙdons his plans to claim LebenôÙsraum for the GerôÙman peoôÙple and to disôÙplace, enslave, or eradôÙiôÙcate GerôÙmanyãs neighôÙbors and undeôÙsirôÙable citôÙiôÙzens. He adopts a posôÙture of non-vioôÙlence and ãuniôÙverôÙsal friendôÙship,ã and GerôÙman forces withôÙdraw from CzechoôÙsloôÙvaôÙkia, Poland, DenôÙmark, France, agreeôÙing to resolve difôÙferôÙences through interôÙnaôÙtionôÙal conôÙferôÙence and comôÙmitôÙtee.
Hitler may have been a vegôÙeôÙtarôÙiôÙan, but thatãs likeôÙly where any symôÙpaôÙthy between him and GandôÙhi began and endôÙed. And yet, the above is preôÙciseôÙly what MahatôÙma GandôÙhi asked of the Fuhrer, in a letôÙter datôÙed DecemôÙber 24, 1940. Engaged fulôÙly in the strugôÙgle for IndiôÙan indeôÙpenôÙdence, GandôÙhi found himôÙself torn by the entry of Britain into the war against GerôÙmany. On the one hand, GandôÙhi iniôÙtialôÙly pledged ãnonôÙviôÙoôÙlent moral supôÙportã for the war, sensôÙing an enemyãGermanyãeven more threatôÙenôÙing to world peace and staôÙbilôÙiôÙty. (That stance would change in short order as the IndiôÙan NationôÙal ConôÙgress revoltôÙed and resigned en masse rather than parôÙticôÙiôÙpate in the war). On the othôÙer hand, GandôÙhi did not see the British Empire as catôÙeôÙgorôÙiôÙcalôÙly difôÙferôÙent from the Nazis. As he put it in his letôÙter to Hitler, whom he addressôÙes as ãFriendã (this is ãno forôÙmalôÙiôÙty,ã he writes, ãI own no foesã): ãIf there is a difôÙferôÙence, it is in degree. One-fifth of the human race has been brought under the British heel by means that will not bear scrutiôÙny.ã
GandôÙhi acknowlôÙedges the absurôÙdiôÙty of his request: ãI am aware,ã he writes, ãthat your view of life regards such spoôÙliôÙaôÙtions as virôÙtuôÙous acts.ã And yet, he marôÙshals a forôÙmiôÙdaôÙble arguôÙment for nonôÙviôÙoôÙlence as a force of powôÙer, not weakôÙness, showôÙing how it had weakôÙened British rule: ãThe moveôÙment of indeôÙpenôÙdence has been nevôÙer so strong as now,ã he writes, through ãthe right means to comôÙbat the most orgaôÙnized vioôÙlence in the world which the British powôÙer repôÙreôÙsentsã:
It remains to be seen which is the betôÙter orgaôÙnized, the GerôÙman or the British. We know what the British heel means for us and the non-EuroôÙpean races of the world. But we would nevôÙer wish to end the British rule with GerôÙman aid. We have found in non-vioôÙlence a force which, if orgaôÙnized, can withôÙout doubt match itself against a comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of all the most vioôÙlent forces in the world. In non-vioôÙlent techôÙnique, as I have said, there is no such thing as defeat. It is all ãdo or dieã withôÙout killing or hurtôÙing. It can be used pracôÙtiôÙcalôÙly withôÙout monôÙey and obviôÙousôÙly withôÙout the aid of sciôÙence of destrucôÙtion which you have brought to such perôÙfecôÙtion. It is a marôÙvel to me that you do not see that it is nobodyãs monopôÙoly. If not the British, some othôÙer powôÙer will cerôÙtainôÙly improve upon your method and beat you with your own weapon. You are leavôÙing no legaôÙcy to your peoôÙple of which they would feel proud. They canôÙnot take pride in a recital of cruôÙel deed, howôÙevôÙer skillôÙfulôÙly planned. I, thereôÙfore, appeal to you in the name of humanôÙiôÙty to stop the war. 
As an alterôÙnaôÙtive to war, GandôÙhi proôÙposôÙes an ãinterôÙnaôÙtionôÙal triôÙbunal of your joint choiceã to deterôÙmine ãwhich parôÙty was in the right.ã His letôÙter, GandôÙhi writes, should be takôÙen as ãa joint appeal to you and SignôÙor MusôÙsoliôÙniãÎ. I hope that he will take this as addressed to him also with the necôÙesôÙsary changes.ã
GandôÙhi also refôÙerôÙences an appeal he made ãto every Briton to accept my method of non-vioôÙlent resisôÙtance.ã That appeal took the form of an open letôÙter he pubôÙlished that July, ãTo Every Briton,ã in which he wrote:
You will invite Herr Hitler and SignôÙor MusôÙsoliôÙni to take what they want of the counôÙtries you call your posôÙsesôÙsions. Let them take posôÙsesôÙsion of your beauôÙtiôÙful island, with your many beauôÙtiôÙful buildôÙings. You will give all these, but neiôÙther your souls, nor your minds. If these genôÙtleôÙmen choose to occuôÙpy your homes, you will vacate them. If they do not give you free pasôÙsage out, you will allow yourôÙself, man, woman and child, to be slaughôÙtered, but you will refuse to owe alleôÙgiance to them.
When GandôÙhi visôÙitôÙed EngôÙland that year, he found the viceroy of coloôÙnial India ãdumbôÙstruckã by these requests, writes StanôÙley Wolpert in his biogôÙraôÙphy of the IndiôÙan leader, ãunable to utter a word in response, refusôÙing even to call for his car to take the now more deeply desponôÙdent GandôÙhi home.ã
GandôÙhiãs 1940 letôÙter to Hitler was actuôÙalôÙly his secôÙond addressed to the Nazi leader. The first, a very short misôÙsive writôÙten in 1939, one month before the ill-fatôÙed SoviôÙet Non-AggresôÙsion Pact, strikes a conôÙcilôÙiaôÙtoôÙry tone. GandôÙhi writes that he resistôÙed requests from friends to pen the letôÙter ãbecause of the feelôÙing that any letôÙter from me would be an imperôÙtiôÙnence,ã and though he calls on Hitler to ãpreôÙvent a war which may reduce humanôÙiôÙty to a savôÙage state,ã he ends with, ãI anticôÙiôÙpate your forôÙgiveôÙness, If I have erred in writôÙing to you.ã But again, in this very brief letôÙter, GandôÙhi appeals to the ãconôÙsidôÙerôÙable sucôÙcessã of his nonôÙviôÙoôÙlent methôÙods. ãThere is no eviôÙdence,ãô The ChrisôÙtôÙian SciôÙence MonôÙiôÙtorô remarks, ãto sugôÙgest Hitler ever respondôÙed to either of GandôÙhiãs letôÙters.ã
As the war unavoidôÙably raged, GandôÙhi redouôÙbled his efforts at IndiôÙan indeôÙpenôÙdence, launchôÙing the ô ãQuit Indiaã moveôÙment in 1942, whichãwrites Open Universityããmore than anyôÙthing, unitôÙed the IndiôÙan peoôÙple against British ruleã and hasôÙtened its evenôÙtuôÙal end in 1947. Non-vioôÙlence sucôÙceedôÙed, improbôÙaôÙbly, against the British Empire, though cerôÙtain othôÙer forôÙmer colonies won indeôÙpenôÙdence through more traôÙdiôÙtionôÙalôÙly warôÙlike methôÙods. And yet, though GandôÙhi believed non-vioôÙlent resisôÙtance could avert the horôÙrors of World War II, those of us withôÙout his levôÙel of total comôÙmitôÙment to the prinôÙciôÙple may find it difôÙfiôÙcult to imagôÙine how it might have sucôÙceedôÙed against the Nazis, or how it could have appealed to their totalôÙizôÙing ideôÙolôÙoôÙgy of domôÙiôÙnaôÙtion.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
MahatôÙma Gandhiãs List of the SevôÙen Social Sins; or Tips on How to Avoid LivôÙing the Bad Life
TolôÙstoy and GandôÙhi Exchange LetôÙters: Two Thinkersã Quest for GenôÙtleôÙness, HumilôÙiôÙty & Love (1909)
Hear Gandhiãs Famous Speech on the ExisôÙtence of God (1931)
Watch GandôÙhi Talk in His First Filmed InterôÙview (1947)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

schema.headline: Gandhi Writes Letters to Hitler: ãWe Have Found in Non-Violence a Force Which Can Match the Most Violent Forces in the Worldã (1939/40)

title: 'Our Neighbors, the Peacocks' Paints a Portrait of an Unusual Convergence of Populations
description: When we think of ãinvasive species,ã perhaps zebra mussels or kudzu vine spring to mind. Both have flourished in their non-native environments and
body:
When we think of ãinvasive species,ã perhaps zebra mussels or kudzu vine spring to mind. Both have flourished in their non-native environments and continue to threaten other native organisms. Invasive species arenãt inherently badãtheyãre just trying to surviveãbut by definition, theyãre likely to disrupt local ecosystems and even cause billions of dollars worth of damage each year. So, what does one California city have to say about its burgeoning population ofãÎ peacocks?
Introduced by a businessman and land baron named Elias Lucky Baldwin more than a century ago, the avian population has long called the area home. Over the years, though, as the originally open area filled with homes and commercial strips, efforts by local residents end up at odds, as some would like to protect the birds and others would prefer to see them sent away altogether. In a short film titled ãOur Neighbors, the Peacocks,ã filmmaker Callie Barlow traces the unusual history of peafowl in Los Angeles County through the eyes of some of its current residents.



Arcadia, California, sits in the San Gabriel Valley about 45 minutes from downtown Los Angeles. Dozens of peacocks, which are carefully watched over by some and detested by others, meander through residentsã yards, traipse around on rooftops, peck at cars, call from trees, and of course, display their beautiful feathersãespecially during mating season.
In her short documentary, Barlow invites locals to share their loveãor loathingãfor the vibrant birds as she highlights how Arcadiaãs history of protecting the birds has perhaps led to something of an overpopulation problem. Nearby neighborhoods participate in relocation programs, while Arcadiaãs birds are protected, and opinions about how to deal with growing numbers land all along the spectrum.
ããOur Neighbors, the Peacocksã resists the urge to resolve the tension it so clearly lays out,ã says Jason Sondhi, who selected the film for Short of the Week. ãInstead, it leans into a modest but resonant idea articulated by its director that living alongside these animals might require ãputting aside your own discomforts to find a deeper meaning in nature.'ã
See the film on YouTube.

schema.headline: 'Our Neighbors, the Peacocks' Paints a Portrait of an Unusual Convergence of Populations

title: Amid Urban Spaces, Alex Senna's Bold Murals Embrace Connection and Belonging
description: In the large-scale murals of Alex Senna, figures gather, greet one another, relax, and interact with their own shadows in bold compositions. The B
body:
In the large-scale murals of Alex Senna, figures gather, greet one another, relax, and interact with their own shadows in bold compositions. The Brazilian artist is known for his black-and-white murals that emphasize community and emotional bonds. Togetherness, security, and positivity pervade the scenes, sometimes playful and other times more contemplative. Set against colorful backgrounds and amid urban structures, Sennaãs pieces emphasize connection, support, reflection, and belonging.
At the end of May, Senna embarks on a tour across Italy, France, and Spain to participate in several festivals. Follow the artistãs Instagram for updates.
Festival Monstar, Bosnia (2022). Photo by Ilda Kero

ãA Estrela de Matusalûˋmã (2022), Festival Gragnano, Italy. Photo by Noemi Verdoliva
Festival Civita Street Fest, Italy. Photo by Alessia Di Risio

ãAtento porûˋm Dispersoã (2024), Kansas City

title: Stained Glass Objects by Pia Hinz Reflect the Contrast Between Strength and Fragility
description: Building sites and agricultural areas are typically described by the utilitarian operations that shape themãrugged, harsh, and often back-breaking
body:
Building sites and agricultural areas are typically described by the utilitarian operations that shape themãrugged, harsh, and often back-breaking. They are spaces that resist softness, built quite literally around force and tension. Artist Pia Hinz flips this idea on its head as she explores the conceptual and material relationship between strength and vulnerability.
Living and working between Ardû´che, Amsterdam, and Arles, France, Hinz has been working with stained glass for the past three years. She focuses much of her work on objects that one might find in environments of labor, such as construction or farming. Her sculptures take on an array of recognizable forms including hammers, screws, traffic cones, tractor doors, scythes, rope, and more.
ãMARTEAUã (2024)
By introducing glass, Hinz subverts the practical purpose of tools and machinery as each object emerges antithetical to its original form. ãHere, fragility and invincibility intertwine,ã says an exhibition statement from La Menuiserie 2, a residency the artist completed in 2024. ãBy replacing functional materials with fragile ones, the artist questions our relationship to objects, their use value, and their narrative potential.ã
As light passes through the artistãs work, it spills across surrounding spaces and results in shifts of color. Pieces like ãMON PRûCIEUXã and ãNûˋon sacrûˋã are elegantly adorned with abstract, geometric patterns that are shaped by winding metal lines. For Hinz, working with the material is, as she describes, an ãurge to retrace the relation between light and space.ã
The artist is currently working on a permanent stained glass monument for a building in Paris. Find more on Instagram.

untitled (2025)
